Fern Polrrrcal PnacrrcEs CouirarssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-s660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
November 18,2005
Mike Fitzpatrick
Anderson City Attorney
Fitzpatrick Law Offices
1135 Pine Street, Suite 107
Redding, CA 96001
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-05-198
Dear Mr. Fitzpatrick:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Mayor Les Baugh
regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (thl..Act,).r
Because your question does not concem a specific governrnental decision, we are treating
your request as one for informal assistance.z This letter should not be construed as advice
on any conduct that.may have already taken place. Finally, our response is based on the
facts presented. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in its advice-giving
capacity. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FppC Ops. 71.)
QUESTION
Does Mayor Baugh have a conflict of interest in decisions which come before the
Anderson City Council if those decisions directly involve Shasta County, which is under
a contract with the Mayor's private company?
CONCLUSION
Shasta County is a source of income to Mayor Baugh by way of the contract
between the county and the mayor's private company. Therefore, absent an exception
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commissioir regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, ofthe California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does notprovide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Government code g 831 14; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. g rs:ie1cy:;.y 
"
eiu,a


File No. I-05-198
Page No. 2
Mayor Baugh may not participate in city council decisions which will have a reasonably
foreseeable, mateial financial effect on the county. However, according to the facts
provided, the decisions in which Mayor Baugh will be called upon to participate will
benefit the county as a whole, and will not uniquely affect the mayor, thus the',public
generally" exception would apply and Mayor Baugh may participate. In the event that
Mayor Baugh is faced with a decision that will affect him or his business uniquely the
"public generally" exception would not apply and he must refrain from participating in
that decision.
FACTS
Mayor Baugh, the Mayor of the City of Anderson, and his wife own a privately
held business located in Anderson. The Mayor's investment in the business is at leasr
$2,000.00. The business has a contract with Shasta County, the county in which the City
of Anderson is located. Under the terms of the contract the Mayor's business is
responsible for building the Shasta County State Fair Exhibit. The contract is for a term
of three years and will likely be renewed. The monetary consideration promised under
the contract exceeds $500.00.
According to your account of the facts, Mayor Baugh will be paid by the cotnty
as an independent contractor through his private business, not as an employee of the
county. You state that he is not considered a "consultant" to the county and he does not
file a Form 700 through the county although he does do so in his capacity as a member of
the Anderson City Council.
The Mayor, in his capacity as city council member, will be called upon to vote on
issues that involve Shasta County. You provide general examples of the types of issues
on which the Mayor may vote. These issues include, "changes in the City;s Sphere of
Interest; Tax Exchange Agreements in connection with annexations; Agreements to share
emergency seryices between the city and county; allocation between city and county of
funds from the State and Federal Governments; etc.,,
AIIALYSIS
A public official may not make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to
use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official
knows, or has reason to know, that he or she has a financial interest. (Section 87100.)
The Commission has adopted a standard eight-step analysis for deciding whether an
official has a disqualiflzing conflict of interest. (Section 87L}3;Regulaiion 1g700(b)(1) _
(8).)
1, Is Mayor Baugh a public official?
The conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to public officials. (Section 87100.)
"Public official" means every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local


File No. I-05-198
Page No. 3
government agency. The City of Anderson is a local govemmental agency. Thus, Mayor
Baugh is a public official under the Act. (Section 82048; RegulationlSZO1 lu;.y
2. will Mayor Baugh be making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only where a public official is
rnaking, participating in making, or is in any way attempting to rrs" hir or her official
position to influence a goveflrmental decision in which he or she knows or has reason to
know the official has a financial interest. (Section g7100.)
The Commission has adopted a series of regulations which define "making,,,
"participating in making," and "influencing" a govemmental decision, and which also
provide certain exceptions. (Regulations 1 8702- I 87 02.4.)
A public official "makes a govemmental decision," when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, appoints a person,
obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, enters into any contracfual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency, or determines not to do any of these things,
unless such determination is made because of his or her financial intirest. (Regulaiion
18702.t(a)(1) - (s).)
You have stated in your request for advice that Mayor Baugh will vote on matters
coming before the city council that directly involve Shasta County. Thus, Mayor Baugh,
will be "making" a govemmental decision.3
3. What are the "economic interests', of Mayor Baugh?
The economic interests that might give rise to a conflict of interest under section
87103 are defined in regulations 18703-18703.5. The specific economic interests
implicated in your request for advice are described below:
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section g7103(a);
Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, purtr"r,
trustee, ernployee, or holds any position of management. (section azto:10;;
Regulation 1 8703. I (b).)
o
3 If a public offrcial is enumerated in section 87200 (including city council members) and he or
she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, thenhe or she must: (l j immediately
prior to the discussion of the item, orally identiff each tJpe of economic interest involved in the decision as
well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in regulation 18702.5,subdivision (b)(l)(B), on the
record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the a*ution'oitt "
discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, "ooserrtia*aars, 
absences and qpeaking as a
member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in regulation \8z)2:5,subdivisions
(c) and (d) apply. (g 8710s).


File No. I-05-198
Page No. 4
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial
effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation I8703.5.)
Investments and Business Positions: A "business entity" is defined in section
82005 as "any organization or enterprise operated for profit, including but not limited to a
proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or
association." Mayor Baugh and his wife own a business located within Shasta County.
Mayor Baugh's business is considered an economic interest because his investment in the
business is worth more than $2,000 and he is an employee of the business. (Sections
87103(a)(c)&(d).)4
Sources of Income: In addition, since the Mayor owns at least l0 percent of the
business, he has an economic interest in each of the customers of the business from
whom he has received income, aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
time any decisions will be made. (Sections 87103(c) and 82030.)t Th" ,orrnty, absent an
exception, as a customer of the Mayor's business, is a source of income to the Mayor.
4. Are the Mayor's economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the decision?
A person, including a business entity or source of income, is directly involved in a
decision before an official's agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request; or,,
a Under regulation 18703.5 the Mayor, like all public officials, has a potentially disqualifying
economic interest in his personal finances. However, neither the financial effects on thi valui of his real
property or his business entity shall be considered when determining whether a goverlmental decision has
a material financial effect on his personal finances. (Regulation 18705.5(a).) Because we are specifically
addressing the Mayor's business entity we will not further address the Mayor's economic interest in his
personal finances.
5 Paynents to a "consultant" for services rendered under contract to a governmental entity are
included within the "government salary exception" of section 82030(bX2). (Regulation 18232.) However,
Mayor Baugh is acting as a general contractor rather than a consultant. Because he does not file a
Statement of Economic Interests (form 700) for the courty, we presume that the county has determined that
Mayor Baugh does not make or participate in the making of government decisions forihe county.
Consequently, the salary exception under section 82030(bX2) would not apply to Mayor Baugh in his
capacity as general contractor. In addition, if the Mayor were a 'oconsultanf ' for purposes of the Acq the
Mayor's economic interest in the business would serve as a separate basis for disqualification. (Section
87103.)
a
a


File No. I-05-198
Page No. 5
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
conceming the decision before the official or the official's agency. A
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person.,, (Regulation
18704.1(a).)"
The Mayor has an economic interest in his private business. Therefore, if the
Mayor's business initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made, the
Mayor's business will be directly involved.
Additionally, if the Mayor's business is a named party, or the subject of the
proceeding concerning the decision, the Mayor's business will also be directly involved
If the business is not directly involved, it is still considered indirectly involved for
pu{poses of determining the appropriate materiality standard. You have given me no
facts that indicate that the Mayor's business has initiated any proceedingr, nor is the
business a named party or the subject of any proceedings before the Mayor. Therefore,
the Mayor's business would be indirectly involved in the decisions that you have
provided as examples in your request for advice.
The Mayor also has an economic interest in Shasta County as a source of income.
The question which determines whether a source of income is directly or indirectly
involved is the same as whether a business entity is directly or indirectly involved. again
the question is, whether the source of income is a named party in, or the initiator or
subject of, the proceedings in which these decisions will occur. A person or entity that is
a source of income is deemed to be indirectly involved if they do not fall into one of the
preceding categories. In your request for advice, you provide limited examples of issues
for which the Mayor will be called upon to vote. The examples provided ari, ,,changes in
the city's Sphere of Influence; Tax Exchange Agreements between the city and the -
county in connection with annexations: Agreements to share emergency services between
the city and county; Allocation between city and county of funds fro- th" State and
Federal Governments; etc." The county is the subject or aparty in each of the examples
provided, thus the county is directly involved.
The Mayor has other sources of income to his business. You provide no facts
indicating whether these other sources would be directly involved in the decisions.
Therefore, we do not provide further analysis as to these potentially disqualifuing sources
of income. You should seek further assistance if there are decisions implicatirg oth"t
sources of income.
5 & 6. Will there be' a reasonably foreseeable material financial effict on the ofJicial's
economic interests?
Once a public official identifies his or her relevant economic interests, the official
must evaluate whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have amateial


File No. I-05-198
Page No. 6
financial effect on any of those economic interests by ascertaining the applicable
materiality standard and then determining whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the
standard will be met. (Regulation 18700(b)(5) &(6), and regulation 18705, et seq.)
Directly Involved: According to regulations 18705.1 and 18705.3, when a person
or business entity is an economic interest to a public official, and is directly involvld in a
decision (as defined above), any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the person or
business entity is deemed material. This standard for determining materiality is known as
the "one penny" rule. If the entity is affected by even one pelmy, the financial effect is
deemed material. This standard would apply to the Mayor's business and the County.
As stated above, il appears that Shasta County is directly involved in the
govemmental decisions that you provided. Thus, any thathas an effect oh the county's
budget by even one penny would be considered a material effect on the Mayor's
economic interest.6
Indirectly Involved: According to regulation 18705.3(b)(1), the materiality
standards for indirectly involved sources of income which are business entities are set
forth in regulation 18705.1(c); and depend on the relative size of the business entity.
Because the Mayor's business is relatively small and not publicly traded, it falls within
the materiality standards set forth in regulation 18705.1(c)(a) (defining the materiality
standard applicable to smaller businesses).
The financial effects of a governmental decision on an indirectly involved smaller
business entity would not be considered material unless the decision under scrutiny
impacted the business as described below:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more." (Reg. 1 8705.1(c)(a).)
The above criteria should be utilized when determining whether the financial
effect on the Mayor's business will be material.
6 Although in this case the materiality threshold is low, the financial effect must still be reasonablv
foreseeable, as discussed below.


File No. I-05-198
Page No. 7
However, the financial effect must also be "reasonably foreseeable". An effect is
considered "reasonably foreseeable" if the effect is "substantially likely." (Regulation
18706; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) A financial effect need not be a certainty
to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In balancing the interests, ifan effect is only a
mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. Whether amatenal financial effect is
reasonably foreseeable at the time a decision is made ultimately depends on facts and
circumstances peculiar to each decision. (In re Thorner, supra.)
You have provided only general examples of the types of govemmental decisions
in which the Mayor will be involved. Thus, we cannot state with particularity whether
the financial effects of each decision would be foreseeable. Should you have a question
regarding a particular decision please provide the fuIl facts and circumstances regarding
that decision in a future request for advice.
However, in the case of the county, you stated that some of the decisions that the
Mayor would be called upon to participate will directly effect the county's budget.
Clearly in those instances, it would be reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect on
the Mayor's economic interest would be material.
7 & 8. Do either the "Public Generally" or the "Legally Required participation,,
exceptions to the conflict-of-interest rules apply?
The "public generally" exception applies when the reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on an official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. (Regulation 18707.) Even if a public official has what
would otherwise be a conflict of interest, he or she is not disqualified if the financial
effect on him or her is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
(Regulation 18707; section 87103; et seq.)
Regulation 18707(a) states, ... "Notwithstanding a determination that the
reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on apublic official,s
economic interests is material, a public official does not have a disqualiffing conflict of
interest in the govefilmental decision if the governmental decision affects the public
official's economic interests in a manner which is indistinguishable from the manner in
which the decision will affect the public generally as set forth in Title2,Califomia Code
of Regulations, sections 1 8707. | -187 07 .9 )' (Regulation 1 8707(a).)
The material financial effect of a govemmental decision will affect a "significant
segment" of the public generally if the decision affects a govenrment entity. (Regulation
18707.1(b)(1XD).) For decisions that affect a federal, state or local government entity in
which the public has an economic interest, the decision will affect all members of the
public under the jurisdiction of that govemment entity. The comment to regulation
18707 .l provides:


File No. I-05-198
Page No. 8
"The term 'affect all members of the public' is intended to cover
decisions affecting the public in general but to exclude decisions which
uniquely benefit a public official."
Your facts indicate that the decisions include "changes in the Sphere of Interest;
Tax Exchange Agreements in connection with annexations; Agreemenls to share
emergency services between the entities; ...." These decisions will benefit the county as
a whole, and will not uniquely affect the mayor. In these types of decisions, the public
generally exception would apply and Mayor Baugh may participate. However, iia
decision uniquely affects the mayor, for example, a decision to amend or renew the fair
exhibit contract, Mayor Baugh would have a disqualifuing conflict of interest. (See e.g.,
Ftsher Advice Letter, No. I-03-001.)
The "legally required participation" rule applies when the official's participation
in a governmental decision is legally required. (Regulation 18708; section SZtOt, 
"opy
enclosed.) You may wish to contact us for further advice if more than one city council
member is disqualified from the above decisions, and the city is prevented from having a
quorum.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-s660.
Sincerely
Luisa Menchaca
General
lttr4
Crowe
Intern, Legal Division

a contract with the Mayor's private company?

person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,

or business entity is an economic interest to a public official, and is directly involvld in a
decision (as defined above), any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the person or
business entity is deemed material. This standard for determining materiality is known as
the "one penny" rule. If the entity is affected by even one pelmy, the financial effect is
deemed material. This standard would apply to the Mayor's business and the County.
As stated above, il appears that Shasta County is directly involved in the
govemmental decisions that you provided. Thus, any thathas an effect oh the county's
budget by even one penny would be considered a material effect on the Mayor's
economic interest.6
Indirectly Involved: According to regulation 18705.3(b)(1), the materiality
standards for indirectly involved sources of income which are business entities are set
forth in regulation 18705.1(c); and depend on the relative size of the business entity.
Because the Mayor's business is relatively small and not publicly traded, it falls within
the materiality standards set forth in regulation 18705.1(c)(a) (defining the materiality
standard applicable to smaller businesses).
The financial effects of a governmental decision on an indirectly involved smaller
business entity would not be considered material unless the decision under scrutiny
impacted the business as described below:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more." (Reg. 1 8705.1(c)(a).)
The above criteria should be utilized when determining whether the financial
effect on the Mayor's business will be material.
6 Although in this case the materiality threshold is low, the financial effect must still be reasonablv
foreseeable, as discussed below.
However, the financial effect must also be "reasonably foreseeable". An effect is
considered "reasonably foreseeable" if the effect is "substantially likely." (Regulation
18706; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) A financial effect need not be a certainty
to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In balancing the interests, ifan effect is only a
mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. Whether amatenal financial effect is
reasonably foreseeable at the time a decision is made ultimately depends on facts and
circumstances peculiar to each decision. (In re Thorner, supra.)
You have provided only general examples of the types of govemmental decisions
in which the Mayor will be involved. Thus, we cannot state with particularity whether
the financial effects of each decision would be foreseeable. Should you have a question
regarding a particular decision please provide the fuIl facts and circumstances regarding
that decision in a future request for advice.
However, in the case of the county, you stated that some of the decisions that the
Mayor would be called upon to participate will directly effect the county's budget.
Clearly in those instances, it would be reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect on
the Mayor's economic interest would be material.
7 & 8. Do either the "Public Generally" or the "Legally Required participation,,
exceptions to the conflict-of-interest rules apply?
The "public generally" exception applies when the reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on an official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. (Regulation 18707.) Even if a public official has what
would otherwise be a conflict of interest, he or she is not disqualified if the financial
effect on him or her is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
(Regulation 18707; section 87103; et seq.)
Regulation 18707(a) states, ... "Notwithstanding a determination that the
reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on apublic official,s
economic interests is material, a public official does not have a disqualiffing conflict of
interest in the govefilmental decision if the governmental decision affects the public
official's economic interests in a manner which is indistinguishable from the manner in
which the decision will affect the public generally as set forth in Title2,Califomia Code
of Regulations, sections 1 8707. | -187 07 .9 )' (Regulation 1 8707(a).)
The material financial effect of a govemmental decision will affect a "significant
segment" of the public generally if the decision affects a govenrment entity. (Regulation
18707.1(b)(1XD).) For decisions that affect a federal, state or local government entity in
which the public has an economic interest, the decision will affect all members of the
public under the jurisdiction of that govemment entity. The comment to regulation
18707 .l provides:
"The term 'affect all members of the public' is intended to cover
decisions affecting the public in general but to exclude decisions which
uniquely benefit a public official."
Your facts indicate that the decisions include "changes in the Sphere of Interest;
Tax Exchange Agreements in connection with annexations; Agreemenls to share
emergency services between the entities; ...." These decisions will benefit the county as
a whole, and will not uniquely affect the mayor. In these types of decisions, the public
generally exception would apply and Mayor Baugh may participate. However, iia
decision uniquely affects the mayor, for example, a decision to amend or renew the fair
exhibit contract, Mayor Baugh would have a disqualifuing conflict of interest. (See e.g.,
Ftsher Advice Letter, No. I-03-001.)
The "legally required participation" rule applies when the official's participation
in a governmental decision is legally required. (Regulation 18708; section SZtOt, 
"opy
enclosed.) You may wish to contact us for further advice if more than one city council
member is disqualified from the above decisions, and the city is prevented from having a
quorum.

QUESTION: Does Mayor Baugh have a conflict of interest in decisions which come before the
Anderson City Council if those decisions directly involve Shasta County, which is under
a contract with the Mayor's private company?

CONCLUSION: Shasta County is a source of income to Mayor Baugh by way of the contract
between the county and the mayor's private company. Therefore, absent an exception
2 Informal assistance does notprovide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Government code g 831 14; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. g rs:ie1cy:;.y 
"
eiu,a
Mayor Baugh may not participate in city council decisions which will have a reasonably
foreseeable, mateial financial effect on the county. However, according to the facts
provided, the decisions in which Mayor Baugh will be called upon to participate will
benefit the county as a whole, and will not uniquely affect the mayor, thus the',public
generally" exception would apply and Mayor Baugh may participate. In the event that
Mayor Baugh is faced with a decision that will affect him or his business uniquely the
"public generally" exception would not apply and he must refrain from participating in
that decision.

Fax (916) 322-0886 November 18,2005 Mike Fitzpatrick Anderson City Attorney Fitzpatrick Law Offices 1135 Pine Street, Suite 107 Redding, CA 96001 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-05-198 Dear Mr. Fitzpatrick: This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Mayor Les Baugh regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (thl..Act,).r Because your question does not concem a specific governrnental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.z This letter should not be construed as advice on any conduct that.may have already taken place. Finally, our response is based on the facts presented. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in its advice-giving capacity. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FppC Ops. 71.) QUESTION Does Mayor Baugh have a conflict of interest in decisions which come before the Anderson City Council if those decisions directly involve Shasta County, which is under a contract with the Mayor's private company? CONCLUSION Shasta County is a source of income to Mayor Baugh by way of the contract between the county and the mayor's private company. Therefore, absent an exception I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Code of Regs. g rs:ie1cy:;.y " eiu,a File No. I-05-198 Page No. 2 Mayor Baugh may not participate in city council decisions which will have a reasonably foreseeable, mateial financial effect on the county. However, according to the facts provided, the decisions in which Mayor Baugh will be called upon to participate will benefit the county as a whole, and will not uniquely affect the mayor, thus the',public generally" exception would apply and Mayor Baugh may participate. In the event that Mayor Baugh is faced with a decision that will affect him or his business uniquely the "public generally" exception would not apply and he must refrain from participating in that decision. FACTS Mayor Baugh, the Mayor of the City of Anderson, and his wife own a privately held business located in Anderson. The Mayor's investment in the business is at leasr $2,000.00. The business has a contract with Shasta County, the county in which the City of Anderson is located. Under the terms of the contract the Mayor's business is responsible for building the Shasta County State Fair Exhibit. The contract is for a term of three years and will likely be renewed. The monetary consideration promised under the contract exceeds $500.00. According to your account of the facts, Mayor Baugh will be paid by the cotnty as an independent contractor through his private business, not as an employee of the county.
